subject: Biology
title: Photosynthesis study guide
description: Understand the role of chlorophyll, light-dependent reactions, and glucose production in a clean revision format.
intro: Photosynthesis is the process plants use to convert light energy into chemical energy. It matters because it links sunlight, plant growth, oxygen release, and the food chain.
keyTakeaways: Photosynthesis mainly happens in chloroplasts, especially inside leaf cells.
Chlorophyll absorbs light and helps start the energy transfer process.
Plants use carbon dioxide and water to build glucose and release oxygen.

sections.heading: What photosynthesis does
sections.content: Photosynthesis stores energy from sunlight in glucose. That stored energy supports plant growth and becomes part of larger food webs.

sections.heading: Inputs and outputs
sections.content: The main inputs are carbon dioxide, water, and light energy. The main outputs are glucose and oxygen.

sections.heading: Why the process matters in exams
sections.content: Photosynthesis often appears in exams through diagrams, equation-based questions, limiting-factor explanations, and comparisons with respiration.

faqs.question: Why is chlorophyll important in photosynthesis?
faqs.answer: Chlorophyll helps absorb light energy so the plant can start the reactions that lead to glucose formation.

faqs.question: Why is oxygen released during photosynthesis?
faqs.answer: Oxygen is released as a by-product during the light-dependent part of the process.

faqs.question: What factors affect the rate of photosynthesis?
faqs.answer: Light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration, water availability, and temperature can all affect the rate.

practiceChecklist: Recall the word equation and the role of chlorophyll.
Explain where photosynthesis happens inside the plant.
Describe one factor that can limit the process.

approvedSources.note: Normalized from classroom-safe teaching notes before publication.

subject: Physics
title: Newton's laws of motion study guide
description: Revise inertia, force, acceleration, and action-reaction pairs with an exam-friendly summary.
intro: Newton's laws of motion explain how objects behave when forces act on them. They are foundational because they connect motion, mass, and force in one framework.
keyTakeaways: The first law explains inertia and balanced forces.
The second law links force, mass, and acceleration.
The third law explains equal and opposite force pairs.

sections.heading: First law
sections.content: An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless an unbalanced external force acts on it. This introduces the idea of inertia.

sections.heading: Second law
sections.content: The rate of change of momentum depends on the applied force. In school-level problems this is often simplified as force equals mass times acceleration.

sections.heading: Third law
sections.content: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction, but the forces act on different bodies.

faqs.question: What is inertia?
faqs.answer: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes to its state of rest or motion.

faqs.question: Why do action and reaction forces not cancel each other?
faqs.answer: They act on different objects, so they do not cancel on the same body.

faqs.question: What is the most common exam mistake with Newton's laws?
faqs.answer: Students often mix up balanced forces with the absence of motion, even though balanced forces can also mean constant velocity.

practiceChecklist: State all three laws in clear language.
Use F = ma correctly in a short problem.
Identify the action-reaction pair in a real-world example.

subject: Chemistry
title: Chemical bonding study guide
description: Review ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding with a structure that is easier to revise from memory.
intro: Chemical bonding explains how atoms combine and why substances have different structures and properties. It is a core topic because it connects microscopic particle behavior with visible material properties.
keyTakeaways: Ionic bonding involves transfer of electrons.
Covalent bonding involves sharing of electrons.
Bond type helps explain melting point, conductivity, and structure.

sections.heading: Ionic bonding
sections.content: Ionic bonding usually happens between metals and non-metals. Electrons are transferred so oppositely charged ions attract each other.

sections.heading: Covalent bonding
sections.content: Covalent bonding usually happens between non-metals. Atoms share electrons to complete stable outer shells.

sections.heading: Metallic bonding
sections.content: In metals, positive ions are held together by attraction to delocalized electrons. This helps explain conductivity and malleability.

faqs.question: Why do ionic compounds usually have high melting points?
faqs.answer: They have strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions in a lattice.

faqs.question: Why can metals conduct electricity?
faqs.answer: Metals have delocalized electrons that can move through the structure.

faqs.question: How can I remember the difference between ionic and covalent bonding?
faqs.answer: Think of ionic as electron transfer and covalent as electron sharing.

practiceChecklist: Compare ionic and covalent bonding in one sentence each.
Link bond type to one physical property.
Explain why metallic bonding supports conductivity.

subject: Mathematics
title: Algebraic expressions study guide
description: Strengthen algebra basics with a clean guide to terms, variables, simplification, and common mistakes.
intro: Algebraic expressions are combinations of variables, numbers, and operations. They matter because they form the language used in equations, identities, and higher-level problem solving.
keyTakeaways: Expressions can include constants, variables, coefficients, and terms.
Like terms can be combined during simplification.
Careful sign handling is essential in algebra.

sections.heading: Parts of an expression
sections.content: A variable represents an unknown value, a coefficient multiplies the variable, and a constant stands alone without a variable.

sections.heading: Simplifying expressions
sections.content: Simplification means combining like terms and using operations correctly. Terms must match in variable part before they can be combined.

sections.heading: Common mistakes
sections.content: Students often combine unlike terms or lose negative signs while expanding and simplifying.

faqs.question: What are like terms?
faqs.answer: Like terms have the same variable part, so they can be combined by adding or subtracting their coefficients.

faqs.question: Can I add x and x squared together?
faqs.answer: No. They are not like terms because the variable parts are different.

faqs.question: Why are negative signs such a common problem?
faqs.answer: Because a sign change can affect every term during subtraction or expansion, so one small mistake changes the whole result.

practiceChecklist: Identify variables, coefficients, and constants in one expression.
Simplify an expression by combining like terms.
Check all signs before writing the final answer.
